data.title: Boeing Vs. Airbus: The Iran War Shock And The Production Reality
data.description:
Airplane manufacturers The Boeing Company (BA) and Airbus SE (EADSF) saw their share prices drop following the war in Iran, which cast a shadow on the economic growth that underpins demand for air travel and subsequently new airplanes. Boeing stock has lost nearly 17% since the invasion and 3% since my last report, in which I discussed the elements that come into play for airplane supply and demand. The European counterpart, Airbus, has lost 20% since the military action in the Middle East and lost 6.5% since my last report.

In this report, I analyze the airplane orders and deliveries for the first two months of the year (March figures are published in April). As mentioned in prior analyses, we're uniquely positioned to provide value estimates on orders and deliveries to more closely reflect the actual benefit to the businesses. While the current stock price performance is predominated by the developments in the Middle East, it remains of utmost importance to assess the production recovery process of Boeing and Airbus programs, as this has been the main bottleneck. At the same time, from the development of airplane orders, we will also be able to see in the months ahead whether there is any softening in order inflow. So, we are seeing that assessing the orders is going to carry more weight.

Below, I have attached the explanation of the analysis methodology from a prior report:

Explanation Of Analysis Methodology For Airplane Orders And Deliveries

For this analysis, I use an internally developed tool available on The Aerospace Forum. I will be assessing the net orders and deliveries. For each, I will discuss the units ordered and delivered and the associated dollar value.

The net orders provide us with a view of demand. This is a reflection of factors such as pricing and availability. Boeing and Airbus have a total backlog of nearly 15,000 airplanes. So, currently, we're not viewing things through the scope of needing more orders to support or increase production. The fact is that the backlogs have to be brought down to healthier levels, and that can be achieved through higher deliveries. Deliveries are what matter, and that's also the area where we see that neither Boeing nor Airbus has been able to support demand. Assessing the deliveries gives us a view of how the recovery for both manufacturers is pacing. As we assess the broader in-year trends, this report considers the year-to-date figures rather than monthly figures on orders and deliveries.

Boeing Extends Lead Over Airbus By Dollar Value

For the first two months of the year, Boeing and Airbus booked a total of 195 net orders, with Boeing collecting 118 net orders compared to 77 for Airbus, giving the US jet maker a 41-unit advantage. Boeing’s net order tally increased by just one order in the single-aisle airplane category, while net orders in the wide-body category increased by 14 units month-on-month. Airbus, on the other hand, booked 28 net orders, taking the lead in the single-aisle category, but did not receive any wide-body orders. So, Airbus currently trails in the wide-body category by 45 units.

In terms of net order value, Boeing’s lead increased from $6.1 billion to $6.6 billion, driven by strength in wide-body order inflow, bringing the net order value for the US jet maker to $10.9 billion, while Airbus increased the net order value by $1.6 billion to $4.3 billion. In the single-aisle category, Airbus leads by $0.4 billion, but the wide-body lead of $7 billion more than offsets that.

Airbus And Boeing Book Net Airplane Orders Value At Highest Level In Years

The net orders for Boeing and Airbus hit 195, marking an 84% increase in net orders, and are at the highest level after the pandemic. We note that net orders have been for the second consecutive year, with Airbus orders growing 18.5% and Boeing orders rising 188%. This gives Boeing a 61% share in the net orders, with the remaining 39% going to its European competitor.

In terms of value, the two jet makers have a combined net order total of $15.2 billion, which marks the highest level recorded since 2019. Just like with the net orders, we see that it is the second consecutive year of increasing net order value, but we note that the mix is in Boeing’s benefit. Airbus’ net order value dropped $2 billion, or 32%, while Boeing’s net order value rose $8.8 billion to $10.9 billion, driven by a strong order mix. This gives Boeing a 72% share in the net order value against 28% for Airbus.

Boeing Remains The Top Producer As Airbus Starts Weak

The first quarter of the year is generally a slow quarter for airplane deliveries, as jet makers try to meet end-of-year delivery deadlines in December, which, combined with the holidays, leaves a small inventory to lean on in the first quarter of the year. Nonetheless, we do see something interesting, and that is while Airbus seemingly has the highest production rates, it is Boeing that is delivering more airplanes even when its airplane inventory of pre-built jets has largely depleted. Boeing and Airbus delivered a total of 151 airplanes in the first two months of the year, with Boeing delivering 97 airplanes and Airbus 54 airplanes. In the single-aisle category, Boeing delivered 80 airplanes, leading Airbus’ 51 single-aisle deliveries by 29 units. In the widebody category, Boeing has a total of 17 deliveries, giving it a lead of 14 units over Airbus and a total lead of 43 airplanes. It is definitely a lead that can still slip, and in March there is the expectation that Boeing 737 MAX deliveries will be soft due to the correction of wiring issues.

In terms of value, Boeing delivered $6.6 billion worth of airplanes compared to $3.2 billion for Airbus, giving it a $3.4 billion lead. $2 billion of the lead is generated in the wide-body segment, while approximately $1.5 billion is generated in the single-aisle segment.

Airplane Production Shows Stability Rather Than Growth But Boeing Is Growing

At a time when airplane manufacturers are looking to rebuild production to record levels, we are seeing a rather unsettling reality, and that reality is that year-on-year deliveries have remained stable and are, in fact, marginally lower. Boeing is on an acceptable track with deliveries up 9% and at the highest level since 2019, but Airbus is really struggling with deliveries down 17% and at the levels last seen in 2021. Airbus’ main issue right now is engine deliveries due to the Pratt & Whitney GTF engine issues, which has led to the prioritization of getting grounded airplanes back in service. The result is that Boeing has a 64% share in the deliveries compared to 36% for Airbus.

In terms of value, both jet makers had a combined delivery output of $9.8 billion, which was down $0.3 billion year-on-year and in line with the first two months of 2020. Boeing grew delivery value by 6.5%, or $0.4 billion, to $6.6 billion, but Airbus’ delivery value slumped 18% to $3.2 billion and now stands slightly above the levels seen in 2021. Boeing has a 67% share in the delivery value compared to 33% for Airbus.

Boeing seems to be catching up with the shortfall in deliveries, having fallen from 50 units to 28 units. Airbus has been having issues consistently improving delivery numbers over the past years, but they seem to be around 35 units behind on what would fit the trend.

Conclusion: Boeing Is Doing Better; Airbus Is Having The Bigger Struggles

At this point, I do believe that Boeing is showing better consistency in production output. There will be a headwind in March, which could help Airbus close the gap somewhat in terms of deliveries, but generally Airbus has been on an extremely underwhelming path to increase production for the past two to three years. The result is that the engineering crisis that Boeing went through has not been as bad as it could have been since Airbus was simply not able to ramp up production as fast as intended. In terms of backlog, both airplane manufacturers have years of production secured, so that is not a major concern. However, at this point airplane manufacturing stocks seem to be a battle of losers, as investors are weighing down valuations of industrials, including aerospace and defense names, to account for a possible macroeconomic softening. While I do believe that is a net negative for Airbus and Boeing, it does give both manufacturers the ability to have a better balance between supply and demand. That is not necessarily a net positive, as it could soften pricing power somewhat. At the same time, we note that jet makers have seen their prices rebound strongly after each and every shock experienced. So, I do remain bullish on the prospects of both jet makers in the long term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHH: It's Not A Hedge Against Push Inflation
data.description:
It's time to revisit the Schwab US REIT ETF (SCHH). I previously covered the ETF in February when I augmented an educational article. Unlike February's coverage, today's article communicated a directional outlook. More specifically, I caution against the expectation that SCHH will provide a hedge against a push inflation-based rally; let's traverse into the analysis.

A Couple Of Starting Points

SCHH In A Nutshell

For those unaware of SCHH, it is a U.S.-based equity REIT ETF. In its own words, “The fund’s goal is to track as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the total return of an index composed of U.S. real estate investment trusts classified as equities,” which simply means the REIT provides investors with systematic access to U.S. equity REITs. In terms of its construction, SCHH follows a float-adjusted market cap weighting scheme and caps its single security exposure at 10%. Moreover, the ETF avoids illiquid issuers by mandating a focus on REITs with market capitalisations above $200 million.

A glance at SCHH ETF's portfolio (Figure 1) shows that its primary sector exposure is health care, retail, and industrial. However, material exposure to telecommunications, residential, data centres, self-storage, and other specialised REITs is also evident—the other data points shown in Figure 1 are zoomed in on later in the article.

In terms of its historical performance. A since-inception chart shows that SCHH has underperformed large-cap U.S. equities (illustrated by the following diagram) since its vintage. Moreover, as illustrated later in the article, cyclicality is evident, making position sizing and timing highly important.

In a nutshell, SCHH provides liquid access to U.S. equity REITs with a slight tilt towards healthcare, industrial, retail, and multifamily REITs. The ETF has underperformed U.S. large-cap equities by a staggering amount since its vintage.

Push vs. Pull Inflation

I often see analysts say, “Inflation is good for real estate,” which is a statement I find baffling, as it treats inflation as homogeneous. Whether inflation benefits real estate or not would depend on the source. For example, inflation can be broken down into demand-pull and cost-push inflation, whereby the former refers to a healthy, strong demand-side economy causing higher price levels, and the latter refers to a supply-side shock, which leads to higher prices. Conventionally, the latter leads to a temporary increase in prices due to the stickiness of demand. However, ultimately, we'll almost always see it cause weakness in the demand side, leading to recession risk (or at least instability among consumers).

Where are we today? Price levels have yet to escalate post-U.S.-Iran. However, I think we are in cost-push territory; I've assumed my stance for three reasons. Firstly, the U.S.-Iran war has led to persistently higher global oil prices and broad-based seaborne supply chain disruptions, which could soon filter into both the producers' and consumers' price index. Furthermore, we have entered an era of global trade fragmentation and potential deglobalisation, which could result in structurally higher input cost bases due to lower emphasis on absolute advantages, relative advantages, and offshoring of labour. Lastly, I think the consumer cycle was on a knife-edge pre-U.S.-Iran—consumer sentiment had already waned significantly. I believe consumers were kept at bay by the Federal Reserve's rate cuts and resilient unemployment numbers, created by residual benefits of a growth in services-based labour opportunities pre- and post-pandemic.

Why SCHH Isn't A Push Inflation Hedge

The Early Signs Are There

SCHH has shed ~7.92% month-over-month, showing that investors essentially priced it as a positive interest rate duration asset. Why would this have occurred? In my view, likely due to investors pricing the possibility of higher interest rates and demand-side weakness, the phenomenon translated into the perception of REITs' asset-liability management (higher liability level, slower rental escalation).

Post-pandemic history shows that a large increase in push-side inflation (2022) was transmitted into a subsequent decline of SCHH's market price. In addition, post-2022's inflation-calming period saw SCHH's price recover, extended by rate cuts and an improved consumer demand side.

Leverage

REITs are leveraged vehicles. For example, if we look at SCHH's three largest constituents, Welltower Inc. (WELL), Prologis, Inc. (PLD), and Equinix, we see Net Debt/EBITDA ratios of 3.03x, 5.3x, and 3.8x. While large REITs usually cover their leverage ratios quite well, their liability levels can reprice if nominal interest rates increase and credit spreads expand (either through floating-rate adjustments or the need for new issuances when existing debt expires). Investors will usually price the prospects of higher financing rates in advance. Although rents can escalate alongside inflation, escalation typically occurs more slowly, and push-side inflation leaves less scope for REITs to escalate without increasing vacancy rates.

Consumer and Industrial Cycle-Dependent

Retail and Industrial REITs can start suffering if the consumer and industrial cycles start deteriorating as a consequence of push-side inflation. In fact, variables such as consumer sentiment already started waning before the U.S.-Iran event, suggesting that headwinds would've occurred in any case.

To supplement the above, I cite the fact that SCHH's top retail/diversified REIT, Realty Income Corporation (O), had recently softened its full-year same-store rental escalation outlook to 1.15% (Figure 5), slightly below the 1.30% achieved in 2025. In addition, it framed a lower occupancy rate of 98.5% (from 98.9% in 2025). Although O REIT is merely one holding in SCHH's portfolio, it acts as a ballast among retail and diversified REITs, meaning its performance echoes the fundamental trajectory of its sectors. In addition to O's softer outlook, I believe industrial REITs will be hit especially hard if we see a continuation in push-side inflation, as will multifamily REITs, given that both are directly linked to the industrial/business cycle.

Side Note: The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) places the odds of a recession at 30%, and Moody's Corporation (MCO) has assigned a 50% probability.

Financial Market Risk Premia

We often forget that REITs are financial assets, meaning their traded performance isn't 1:1 with fundamentals. Investors go through various risk regimes. A risk-off regime typically occurs when inflation is high and unstable, which can lead to a sell-off in risky assets such as REITs. As illustrated by the following figure, the S&P VIX Index (VIX) has been relatively subdued since the U.S. Federal Reserve started cutting interest rates in 2024. However, the instability of recent macro variables has led the VIX higher, arguably revealing complacency by risk-on investors. Assuming persistence in the geopolitical instability, we could see the VIX structurally higher for a while, leading to a spike in risk market premiums.

The Dividend Doesn't Provide Much Drawdown Coverage

Many invest in REITs for dividend safety. However, it should be noted that SCHH has a substantial historical max drawdown of 44.22%, suggesting a dividend won't meaningfully soften the blow if a left-tail risk event occurs via excessive push inflation.

Valuations Are Already Steep

SCHH's price-to-book ratio of 2.25x is already quite high for a REIT vehicle, considering the fact that REITs are priced on a tangible asset base. For a more granular view, I embedded SCHH's top 10 holdings' price/book ratios below. Some might disagree, but I think price-to-book ratios are cyclically high, given the strong run-up in REITs during the Federal Reserve's easing cycle—paired with a portfolio aggregate 5-year earnings growth rate of merely 4.35%, I fail to see the ETF's current price level being sustainable. I think the market might look for reasons to sell expensive REITs if a bear market narrative manifests.

Conclusion

I think the events in Iran will have a lasting impact on inflation based on higher oil prices, supply chain disruptions, and the general fragmentation of global trade. Although the Schwab US REIT ETF might hedge against demand-side inflation, its use case as a push inflation hedge is suboptimal. Moreover, SCHH's multiples are relatively high, industry leverage will be debated if bond yields stay pent-up, and the underlying earnings growth of SCHH's constituents is moderate.

I'm actively looking for opportunities within the oversold individual REITs sphere. However, I'm not going to engage with SCHH, especially not conditional on it being an inflation hedge.

data.title: Alaska Air Group: Double Downgrade On Oil Price And Macro Risk
data.description:
Alaska Air Group (Alaska Airlines) (ALK) stock is up 7.2% as the sentiment is largely positive on the broader market. The uptick in the share price comes a day after the company updated its guidance; while the release reads somewhat positively, it is not a positive adjustment to the expectations. In this report, I discuss the updated guidance and update my price target for Alaska Airlines, as I can no longer maintain the strong buy rating issued in October 2025.

Alaska Airlines Plagued by High Oil Prices

On the 30th of March, Alaska Airlines issued a guidance update, and while the company does highlight demand strength, I do believe the outlook is much more uncertain in the balance of the year. The company noted that demand continued to show strength, with unit revenues tracking in line with prior expectations. Into the second quarter, forward bookings 90 days out are up 25% for corporate travel, with yields and load factors also up.

The company did note that fuel costs have increased materially. Alaska Airlines always has some disadvantages when it comes to fuel costs, and the fuel price environment is now evolving in a negative way with refining margins increasing. The company now expects economic fuel prices to be $2.90-$3.30, providing a $0.70 headwind, with additional headwinds in the form of storms in Hawaii and cartel violence in Mexico leading to softer demand. The company noted that absent fuel and demand disruptions, the guidance would have been met at the midpoint of the range, but including disrupting factors, the company now expects a loss per share between $1.50 and $2.00. Previously, the company expected a loss per share of $0.50-$1.50. So, at the lower end there was a $0.50 downward shift, and at the higher end of the range, a $1.00 shift at the high end with a midpoint shift of $0.75, largely reflective of the oil price headwind.

On the demand and yield side, Alaska Airlines seems to be upbeat. However, we note that with unit revenues tracking as expected for Q1, they did not really absorb the higher fuel prices, which are typically partially passed through if the demand environment allows it. So, that increases pressure in the second quarter, and from those comments, it seems that demand is robust and yield is up. However, the company only quantified corporate travel demand trends

Analysts Have Revised Estimates Down

For Q1, analysts have revised their revenue estimates up by less than a percent to $3.32 billion, with EPS revised down from -$0.84 to -$1.05. For the second quarter, revenue estimates have been revised up by 1.3%, while EPS estimates have fallen by 35.2%. We do note that this decline has been consistent over the one-month, three-month, and six-month windows. It is not necessarily an indication that the war pressure is not fully priced in, but it is a risk. More importantly, the annual figures show that revenues are still expected to rise 9.3% with a 1.3% upward revision over the past month, but EPS estimates have been revised down by 20.6%. It gives the impression that there is extremely limited space for Alaska Airlines to pass through higher fuel costs, or at least a limited pass-through is being modeled right now. So, the risk remains that elevated fuel prices and associated macro risk may inflict further downside to the estimates.

Alaska Airlines is Undervalued, but for a Reason

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Alaska Airlines, I am adjusting my rating from strong buy to hold. We note that at the company’s 3-year median EV/EBITDA, the stock remains undervalued against the peer group, but right now we see major risk in the form of elevated oil prices and macroeconomic pressures that could affect demand this year and into the next year. So, while there is upside, I am downgrading the stock. We note that EBITDA estimates for 2026 have come down by 22%, and for 2027 estimates have remained stable, but there is downward pressure on those estimates driven by macroeconomic growth risks. So, if you want to buy this, it would be more of a contrarian buy, betting on the war ending over the next couple of days.

Margins are expected to dip this year and rebound in the next year, but whether that rebound will actually materialize will depend on the state of the global economy, which at this point is highly uncertain. Cash conversion remains highly irregular, driven by a combination of demand and timing of capital expenditures. The net debt leverage is expected to remain within an acceptable range this year, but the cash pile is set to decline, meaning that additional debt could be added.

Conclusion: Alaska Airlines is Not Looking Attractive

Solely looking at the current estimates, Alaska Airlines is looking somewhat attractive. However, I do believe that there is additional downward pressure on the earnings estimates for this year and the coming years, mostly driven by a potential softening of demand due to macroeconomic growth tapering. In terms of fuel prices, my expectation is that there is also additional risk there, primarily this year. Alaska Airlines does not hedge its fuel exposure. The company previously used crude oil call options to hedge fuel exposure (10K, Page 9), but all positions were settled in 2025 with no hedge positions by the end of 2025.

Given the combination of macroeconomic growth risks and the oil price environment, I do believe it is prudent to downgrade the stock to hold.

data.title: South Korea's Economy Benefits From Robust Chip Exports And Fiscal Support
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Exports jumped in March thanks to surge in chip exports

South Korean exports rose 48.3% year-on-year in March (vs 29% in February, 44.8% market consensus). Among 15 major export items, 10 items gained, largely due to favourable price effects. Semiconductor exports rose an impressive 151.4%, building on the 160.6% rise in February and 102.7% gain in January. Semiconductor exports jumped 139% YoY in the first quarter. Rising chip prices led to sharp increases in exports of computers and SSDs in March, up 189% and 218%, respectively. We expect strong demand for AI and memory chips to continue, with no significant signs of a slowdown in AI investment globally. But clearly, this is likely to add to inflationary pressures on IT goods globally – and, eventually, more burdens for consumers.

To date, semiconductors haven’t experienced significant shortages of raw materials. However, inventories of essential materials are expected to be depleted within the next few quarters. If supply disruptions persist, adverse effects could become evident in the second half of 2026. Given Korea’s high dependence on chips for growth, negative impacts could intensify later this year.

Chip exports surged while other exports also improved in 1Q26

Oil and petrochemical rise, exports expected to decline from April

We observe that recent increases in oil prices boosted petroleum and petrochemical exports in March, up 54.9% and 5.8%, respectively. However, the data showed that exports of gasoline, diesel, and kerosene fell in volume in the later part of March. The decline is mainly due to the implementation of export controls on 13 March.

Price effects worked favourably for now. However, since Korean exporters rely heavily on Middle Eastern products, we are more cautious about the export outlook for these sectors in the near term. The government has banned the exports of Naphtha and tightly controlled oil products since mid-March. The government is considering an emergency decree to manage key raw materials. We see a higher probability that short-term exports of oil and chemicals may be interrupted by these controls and supply disruptions.

Other than these two sectors, automobile exports rebounded 2.2%, following a 20.1% drop in February. Home appliances (-7.7%) and steel (-2.2%) also continued to drop, which seems more related to the US tariffs and weak global demand.

By export destination, exports to China and the US rose the most – by 64.2% and 47.1%, respectively. This was mostly driven by strong IT exports. Meanwhile, exports to the Middle East dropped 49.1% due to the ongoing war.

Imports growth accelerated in March

Imports rose 13.2% YoY in March (vs 7.5% in February, 15.3% market consensus). Despite the surge in crude oil prices, energy imports fell by 5%, mostly due to lower import volumes. Non-energy imports – semiconductors (34.8%) and chip-making equipment (4.4%) - rose 17.9%. The trade surplus widened quite significantly to $25 billion from the previous month’s $15 billion.

Government's supplementary budget to reduce Middle East shock

The government proposed a 26.2 trillion won ($17.3 billion) extra budget to mitigate Middle East shocks. The spending plan features cash payments and energy subsidies for lower-income households and small business owners, as well as compensation for oil refiners affected by fuel price caps. Shopping vouchers ranging from 100,000 to 600,000 won ($70 to $400) per person will be distributed based on income. The extra budget won’t require additional debt issuance. Instead, the government would utilize excess tax revenues from robust chip exports and strong stock market performance. The government also offered 1 trillion won worth of KTB redemption to stabilise the market.

The extra budget will lift government spending in 2026 to 752.1 trillion won, an 11.8% year-on-year increase, projected to raise GDP by 0.2 percentage points. Both parties agreed to approve the bill by 10 April. If it passes, it may help support growth mostly in the second quarter of 2026.

GDP is expected to grow 2.0% YoY in 2026, yet downside risks are increasing

Solid March exports and a firm recovery in investment from yesterday's monthly industrial production data suggested that 1Q26 GDP would rebound by 0.6% quarter-on-quarter, seasonally-adjusted (vs -0.2% in 4Q25). While consumption may have weakened with rising energy prices and severe corrections in equity markets, exports and investment should boost overall growth. The latest data showed that the Iran war's impact is limited for first-quarter growth.

However, we expect the growth to slow more than expected in 2Q26. The disruption of supplies will dampen activity in the manufacturing sector and put more cost pressure - even if the war ends within a couple of weeks. At the same time, government-funded fiscal aid is likely to play a critical role in limiting the negative impact. Thus, we expect growth to decelerate to 0.2% QoQ but to avoid a contraction in 2Q26. Along with trimming down the 2Q26 and 3Q26 GDP, we have lowered our 2026 GDP outlook from 2.2% YoY to 2.0%. This implies that Korea's growth remains above potential.

The March consumer price inflation data will be released tomorrow. We expect CPI inflation to rise to 2.5% YoY, slightly above the market consensus of 2.3%. Despite the government fuel price cap and a further fuel tax cut, petrol prices are expected to rise, while the recent sharp rise in import prices is expected to add pressure on broad goods prices. Higher energy prices for longer and supplementary budget measures could increase upward inflation risks in coming months.

If we are right about the resilience of the economy and a higher-inflation path, then the Bank of Korea's policy focus will be on inflation stabilisation and financial stability. We expect the Bank of Korea to deliver a 25bp hike in July under the new governor, Shin Hyun Song.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: IBEX Limited: A Hidden Growth Engine In Healthtech
data.description:
IBEX Limited (IBEX) traditionally operated as a service contractor or business process outsourcing, but recently the company decided to expand into the HealthTech sector to manage the revenue cycle for medical entities, which could drive the market to re-rate it as a tech company and give it higher multiples in the long term.

The healthcare sector in the United States is among the most complex sectors, as when a hospital treats a patient, it collects its money through insurance companies such as Medicare or Medicaid. However, this process is going through hard steps, including registering patient data and reviewing his insurance policy to make sure it covers his treatment and dealing with refusals of insurance companies to the hospital claims until it finally collects the bills. In addition, these processes that are full of human errors and costing healthcare providers billions of wasted money annually due to poor revenue management, so Ibex realized this problem and decided to work on it through relying on trained labor in the medical sector from higher margin offshore locations such as India, as it employed 1,000 employees providing RCM services, allowing it to take advantage of the currency difference as it pays employees' salaries in rupees and collecting revenues in US dollar.

Importantly, it integrated this workforce with its AI-powered Wave iX platform to reduce human errors coming from manual work, as it uses AI to accelerate routine tasks such as reviewing insurance policies and patient files to minimize the claims rejections from insurance companies and improve accuracy, and in the event of a tough issue or insurance rejection, an employee intervenes to handle the issue. This should provide hospitals with faster cash flows and reduce their cash burnout. This appeared in Q2 results, as the HealthTech segment recorded growth of 35% YoY, as it now represents 17.4% of the company's total revenues compared to 15% last year. Also, the CEO stated that this segment alone will make 100 million by the end of the year, which suggests that the company is changing its DNA and gradually focusing on higher profit segments.

The BPO Segment

In 2024, the company announced its Wave iX Translate solution, which won the Best GenAI Product from TMC. This product provides real-time translations for over 150 different languages. This means that the company no longer needs native speakers thanks to this revolutionary product, which enables the company to hire low-cost non-native speakers from developing countries without customers feeling any difference. This will lead to a dramatic reduction in the cost of sales, allowing the company to offer competitive prices for customers and increase its market share plus raise its profit margins.

Financial Results & Outlook

During Q2 of 2026, the company achieved revenues of $164.2 million with growth of 16.7% YoY. This is an excellent figure. But what's impressive is that the company achieved this growth for the fourth quarter in a row, in light of the BPO sector declining by 14% in 2025, as this was the worst year for the sector since 2020, indicating that the company's growth was primarily driven by gaining market share, reflecting the customer confidence in the company and the quality of service it offers.

The company also recorded strong growth of 20.2% in the logistics segment, as well as 17.2% in the retail and e-commerce. However, the telecom declined by 23.1%, which was expected as the management tries to reduce its exposure to low-margin segments to focus on high-return ones.

The adjusted EBITDA improved by around 80 basis points to reach $20.7 million, or 12.6% of revenues, versus 11.8% YoY, as the company relies on low-cost locations such as India and the Philippines; besides, it focuses on higher-profit sectors, as we previously mentioned.

However, the company recorded a negative free cash flow of $-5.1 million vs. $-3.2 million last year, but the net cash from operations was positive with $6.6 million vs. $1.1 million only last year. The reason is that the company spent $11.7 million to expand its operations in India and employ 1,000 people to support its strategy in the HealthTech and RCM sectors.

IBEX has a liquidity of $15.5 million and almost negligible debt of $1.4 million. Which gives it the flexibility to invest in tech or buy back shares without pressure from creditors.

Furthermore, it has raised its guidance for 2026, so the revenues are expected to be between $620 and $630 million and the adjusted EBITDA between $80 and $82 million, reflecting the management's confidence in the deals it has entered and signaling a green light for the company's growth.

Valuation

IBEX has a number of direct competitors, such as Concentrix and TTEC. They trade at a close EV/EBITDA of each other. However, IBEX is growing faster and expanding in the RCM sector, which witnessed a growth of 35%. Therefore, it deserves to be evaluated at a higher multiple than its peers. Considering the company's guidance of around $80 million in 2026, the target EV will be around $400 million, and after adding the cash reserves and subtracting debts, the market cap is going to be $410 million, which represents a potential upside of 15%.

However, if the company executes its strategic pivot toward HealthTech and high-margin sectors, the market will eventually recognize this shift and consequently reward it with higher multiples.

The AI & Concentration Risks

The company faces a major challenge, which is that generative AI is going to replace customer and call center services with AI agents and consequently cause a revenue collapse for outsourcing companies that price their services in ACV contracts based on the number of employees and hours. However, this risk is logical for the companies that rely solely on manual labor. Fortunately, Ibex is trying to benefit from AI rather than considering it a threat, as it tries to innovate new solutions using AI, such as Wave iX Translate, which significantly reduces COGS and benefits from low-cost offshore locations. It can also change the pricing method to be based on outcomes, not just hours. This is in addition to the company's investment in the health tech, which deals with strict regulations and confidential medical data that requires human review. This should act as a buffer against the risk of complete automation, at least in the short term.

The company also relies on a small number of major clients, as the top ten customers represent about 57% of total revenues; there is even a single client that represents about 10% of total revenue. Therefore, if this client decides to reduce or even terminate his engagement with the company, this would severely impact IBEX’s revenues. However, the company's retention rate exceeds 98%, indicating strong relationships with its clients, plus the continued revenue growth should gradually reduce the percentage of these clients to be more sound and mitigate this risk.

Final Thoughts

IBEX achieved excellent results during the first half of 2026, plus its smart expansion in India and moving towards high-profit segments such as RCM are strong catalysts that justify giving it a buy rating. Nevertheless, during the next quarter, we should keep an eye on the growth of the HealthTech segment and whether the company can maintain the same rate of 35%, which will be an indicator of the success of IBEX's strategy and a key driver for future growth.

data.title: Nutex Health: Attractive Multiples Hide A Volatile Core Business
data.description:
Nutex Health (NUTX) is at a spot where investors have to look hard at just how much the company's profits can grow and, at the same time, how exposed the business is if things go wrong. I'm still basically neutral on Nutex Health. "Hold" makes sense to me, not because the stock looks cheap on those current multiples (even though they do look pretty tempting at first glance), but because the whole situation depends so much on details: the weaknesses in the business model, the things that could go sideways, and the way the rules work right now. For 2025, revenue shot up 82% to $875.3 million, and gross profits hit $444.3 million; that's over a 50% margin.

Earlier, I was cautious, for good reason, about how well Nutex could actually scale up and handle things before IDR really took off. But the latest results are hard to ignore: patient visits grew by 11.8% (now at 188,300), the hospital side saw better margins, and the company kept winning more than 85% of its cases in arbitration.

Still, there's a catch. Most of that growth is coming from one place. About 63% of the hospital side's revenue, so around $527.8 million, is directly tied to appeals and disputes settled under IDR. That's actually down 7% from the last quarter, but it's still a huge chunk. Now, the company sends about half of all its claims to arbitration, down from 60%. That shows they're being more careful about which disputes they pursue, but it also hints there's only so far this approach can go. The whole setup is still pretty dependent on a single legal-financial strategy.

Business Model

Nutex Health mostly stands out because of how it runs its micro-hospitals and gets paid out-of-network, going after high-acuity cases that move fast, cases where it can push back against insurers' usual limits by using the IDR process. The company gets some extra stability from its tech-driven population health business, but in 2025, that side only brought in $31 million out of the total, with pretty much all EBITDA still coming from the hospitals. More patients coming in, working the IDR system hard, and tighter costs together pushed Adjusted EBITDA to $259.6 million in 2025, which is more than double last year. Net income for Nutex's shareholders landed at $70.8 million. Management put it this way: "Even with 3 new hospitals opening in 2025 and early 2026, the current portion of long-term debt decreased slightly from $14.4 million to $13.3 million. Net long-term debt increased from $22.5 million to $29.2 million, still very low relative to our revenue and expansion pace." They're not just posturing; cash on hand jumped to $186 million from $41 million a year earlier, and operating cash flow for 2025 came in at $248.1 million. Still, that financial cushion isn't as big as it looks once you set it next to $340 million in total debt, stock-based comp that hit $117 million this year, and the simple math that shows how much they rely on winning those IDR claims to keep the money coming in.

Industry Context

Zooming out, the industry makes things even trickier. Most hospitals have to deal with reimbursement headaches and swings in patient traffic, but hardly any have as much riding on IDR revenue or legal risk as Nutex does. The more established hospitals, where visits barely grew, just 1.3% up from last year, help smooth out the numbers from the newer locations, and keeping that "base" steady is going to matter a lot if arbitration starts to bring in less. Lately, Nutex's updates have been a mix of operational progress and cleanup, with press releases highlighting earnings, hospital openings, and buybacks alongside ongoing legal investigations and routine corporate updates. The company says it "remediated all previously disclosed material weaknesses in internal controls over financial reporting in 2025", which is good, but it's not really something that brings in more cash or makes the risks go away.

Valuation Gap

Nutex's 2025 numbers look strong on paper: $70.8 million net income, $10.48 a share, Adjusted EBITDA margin close to 30%, and $248.1 million cash from operating activities. The numbers make the stock look cheap, trading at just 3.5x EV/EBITDA and 5.1x forward PE. If you run those multiples against what hospitals usually get, you could argue the stock should be much higher, especially if arbitration payouts hold up and patient visits keep growing at mature hospitals. Even a 5x EBITDA multiple on Nutex's current $259.6 million would mean the stock could go up by more than 100% from here. But that kind of math misses some big risks, like how fragile the IDR model actually is and how much Nutex relies on legal workarounds to keep its margins high.

Volatility Signals

Regulation and lawsuits are still hanging over the story. Results bounce around from quarter to quarter, mostly because of when arbitrations get settled and one-off stuff, like the $55 million revenue hit in Q4, when almost 19,000 arbitration claims got knocked out because CMS said they didn't count for backlog clearance. They spelled it out: "Excluding the impact of this adjustment, our Q4 2025 adjusted revenue would be approximately $206.7 million, which is consistent and in line with revenue levels from previous quarters." If Nutex can keep converting cash at 85%+ on those arbitration wins and keep margins in the high teens for another year, that would be a big sign their model is hanging together. But right now, if there's another big revenue restatement or the law changes, profits from arbitration could get chopped. There's no way to make that up through normal operating improvements.

The risks here aren't just about Nutex; they're built into the system. If regulators tighten up who qualifies for the IDR program or cap what can be paid out through arbitration. Management has warned that without IDR, hospitals could "have the unchecked ability to dictate payments unilaterally, effectively determining winners and losers in the marketplace and undermining fair competition." That's the core threat. The market is also still wary because of past issues, restated financials, high stock-based pay, and late filings. That's kept the stock cheap, and just bringing in more patients by itself won't fix that.

For 2026, it all depends on the next set of events. To move from Hold to Buy, I'd need to see either clear rules that keep IDR cash flows safe or a solid, lasting pickup in patient visits that doesn't rely on arbitration, proven out over a few straight quarters with mature hospitals still showing margin above 25%.

Hold Still Makes Sense

Bottom line, Nutex Health has delivered better operations, but the risks still outweigh the upside for a Buy right now. The business is tied to legal and regulatory stuff that could cut cash flow if things swing the wrong way. The stock looks cheap, but that's because of very real risks on both sides. The only way to really earn a higher multiple is to show the business works without relying on IDR windfalls by growing patient visits, making better use of fixed costs, and getting some regulatory certainty. I'm sticking with a Hold. Until those risks start to clear, my view on Nutex Health doesn't really move much.

data.title: McCormick And Unilever Foods: Not A Happy Merger Yet
data.description:
The investment case surrounding McCormick (MKC) changed overnight as the company entered into a complicated deal with Unilever PLC (UL) to merge with selected parts of Unilever's food business.

The deal is rather complicated, in which Unilever and its shareholders get the majority of the shares, but control seems to be with McCormick, which is the surviving entity. Moreover, the business will grow its revenues by 150%, all while organizational complexity will rise substantially.

The deal is about becoming a giant in flavors and foods, and while exchange rates look fair, investors fear a leverage buildup, realization of synergies, and a poor track record of mega food mergers.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

A Complicated Deal

McCormick and Unilever have entered into a complicated agreement under which McCormick will combine with Unilever's Foods business, excluding the operations in India and some other businesses. The deal is designed to create a $20 billion giant in flavor and food.

The deal is rather complicated. Unilever and its shareholders are expected to obtain a 65% equity share in the combination while obtaining $15.7 billion in cash as well. This values the Unilever business at $44.8 billion, equal to 13.8 times EBITDA. The same EBITDA multiple has been applied to McCormick, which hereby is valued at $21.0 billion.

Note that Unilever is not necessarily the controlling entity. Of the 65% equity ownership, some 55.1% appears to be spun off directly to investors in Unilever, with the company owning just 9.9% of the outstanding shares itself.

The strategic rationale is driven by scale and diversification, combining Unilever's iconic brands like Knorr and Hellmann's with McCormick's brands like French's and Frank's RedHot. Moreover, McCormick's strength in flavor and spices is combined with Unilever's brands and distribution scale.

Other benefits include two culturally led organizations, a global footprint with complementary attributes, and annual cost synergies seen at $600 million each year, to be fully realized the year after closing. Such synergies are seen as equal to about 3% of pro forma revenues, around $20 billion each year.

Pro Forma Implications

The $20 billion business is posting pro forma operating income around 21%, with synergies having the potential to boost this number to levels around 24% of sales. The combination touts the importance of flavor, with taste and flavor being key, seeing appeal to younger age cohorts while being aligned to health and wellness.

Unilever Foods is contributing about $12 billion in these sales with underlying operating margins reported at 24%, while McCormick generates about $8 billion in sales, with margins reported around 17%.

McCormick ended the first quarter with $4.8 billion in net debt; this number is seen increasing to $20.5 billion if we factor in a $15.7 billion cash payment to Unilever. With pro forma EBITDA seen at $4.8 billion, excluding synergies, leverage is seen at 4.3 times ahead of deleveraging efforts ahead of the deal. Leverage is seen coming down to 3 times just two years post the deal closing.

The company ended the quarter with some 269 million shares outstanding, and given a 35% pro forma share count, I peg the pro forma share count at 769 million shares. Based on the one-month volume-weighted average price of $57.84 per share, the market value of the combination is seen around $44.5 billion, for a $65 billion enterprise valuation.

So far the math, as the question is what the pro forma impact is. While the news about the deal did not come out of the blue, shares of McCormick fell about 6% in response to the deal, with shares down $3, losing about $2.2 billion in value (including shares to be issued). Shares of Unilever fell by a similar percentage, with investors in both firms being negatively surprised by the news here.

What Now?

Shares of McCormick have fallen from $70 to $50 in the time frame of just a few weeks, that is, during the month of March. That, of course, came amidst market uncertainty surrounding events in the Middle East, rumors about an imminent deal, and the negative reaction in response to the deal announcement.

Currently, shares have fallen to the lowest levels since 2018, as investors are skeptical. While the multiples applied to both businesses are similar, the market is skeptical about the added near-term leverage, realization of synergies, and, in general, the working of mega-deals in food (or, better said, the not working of them). After all, the food industry has a terrible track record in this, as this case will not be easy given operations across segments, continents, and cultures here.

McCormick was on track to post earnings just north of $3 per share on an adjusted basis. While the pro forma implications are not entirely known, doing a transaction with a similar EBITDA multiple should, in theory, result in similar earnings numbers. Some upside is seen, if not for the fact that quite some debt is incurred on which some earnings per share accretion is likely seen.

Delivering on synergies is key, as a $600 million synergy target is equal to about $0.80 per share on a pre-tax basis, leaving a roadmap for earnings to advance to about $3.50-$3.75 per share down the road. Of course, this results in lower valuation multiples here, but in the near term, leverage will increase quite a bit.

Moreover, a big transaction like this, with global operations and difficult cultures, requires some real integration efforts, as success is far from a given. Given all this, I am interested given the fundamental valuation and the setback in the share price, but the concerns about this deal make me rather cautious here.

This makes me sit this one out for now, making me willing to learn more about timing, leverage, and pro forma earnings per share numbers as more news gets known.

data.title: EM Corporate Debt: 5 Themes To Watch In 2026
data.description:
In 2025, emerging markets (EM) corporate credit delivered another solid year, with index returns of 8.7%. The asset class outperformed developed markets (DM) corporate credit across all ratings except CCCs, although it underperformed an exceptional year in EM sovereign debt.

We highlight five themes we believe must be evaluated to better navigate the EM corporate debt universe in the coming year.

Theme 1: The Commodities Opportunity Set in EM Corporate Debt

The significant rally in metals, particularly precious, continues to grab headlines, while crude oil markets remain volatile as they price the uncertain impacts of geopolitical events. When we invest in commodity issuers, it is important to understand what our universe offers and how commodity prices actually affect a company’s credit quality and its bond valuations.

The metals and mining sector accounts for 6% of the J.P. Morgan Corporate Emerging Market Bond Index (CEMBI) Broad Diversified (BD)[1]. This sector includes large-cap, integrated, multi-metal, and multi-mine issuers as well as smaller companies that are less diversified by product and asset base. One can get exposure to mature, world-class mining operations but also to growing businesses with smaller footprints. Parsing the sector by metal is nuanced as mines often market and bundle byproducts within their core operations.

Metals and Mining Sector Breakdown in CEMBI BC

A breakdown of the oil and gas sector reveals a universe not purely driven by volatility in oil prices. Almost two-thirds of the 11% sector weight in the index can be classified as integrated oil companies. These are large issuers that operate across the hydrocarbon value chain, not only exploring and producing oil and natural gas but also transporting it, refining it, producing petrochemicals, and distributing fuels. Every step in this value chain balances the direct oil-price risk, creating a more stable sector than headline prices would indicate.

Oil and Gas Sector Breakdown In CEMBI BD

Commodity prices are the top-line input into an issuer’s financial profile, but just like in a pinball machine, the path to credit quality is not direct. High commodity prices undoubtedly grow revenues, but it is the issuer’s usage of cash flows that needs to be analyzed.

It is also worth noting that a majority of the upside in commodity prices accrues to shareholders' equity, while bond spread returns can be bounded by relative value considerations. This is not to say that high commodity prices don’t tend to improve the credit quality of issuers, but rather that it is up to diligent investors to understand the ultimate impact on credit risk.

Conversely, bond prices have more convexity[2] as prices slide. This mostly occurs when oil prices hit the perceived full-cycle cost[3] of the issuer and viability of operations is questioned. A company’s flexibility to withstand lower price periods ultimately defines where on the credit quality spectrum the pinball ultimately lands.

We believe understanding this opportunity set, as well as the reality of how commodity prices affect individual issuers, is crucial to our positioning in commodity corporate credit investments.

Theme 2: Private Issuers in EM Corporate Debt

Lately, growth in private credit has garnered considerable interest in the markets. Adding a different source of capital for issuers is positive, yet headline-grabbing defaults, significant fund markdowns, and comments suggesting weak underwriting standards can put a dent in the perceived low volatility of these investments. The periodic nature of pricing can also mask increases in credit risk and lead to stepwise (sometimes shocking) adjustments, while the ability to renegotiate agreements in times of credit stress could muffle underlying default trends.

We see two potential paths in private credit: one where investors are essentially company insiders, with deeper and more timely access to information than public investors, leading to improved diligence and thus not posing a macro risk to markets; and another where private credit has become a way for issuers to access deep, fast, and less demanding pools of capital, where concerns about the state of the asset class are then warranted.

Private credit in EMs is still nascent, and extracting the use of private credit by EM issuers is complex, so we take a different approach. By analyzing private issuers in the index, we can get a sense of the risk of private markets in EM corporate debt. Major categories of private issuers in our universe can be defined as privately held issuers, unconsolidated (solo or joint) ventures of public companies, and project finance special purpose vehicles.

When private issuers access public markets, they can be perceived as less transparent, although they must abide by the same disclosures and requirements as public issuances. Over time, if engaging appropriately with the investment community, private issuers can overcome this perception, creating a potential opportunity for investors.

We believe analytical access and company transparency is indispensable for appropriate investment diligence, and we engage with issuers to encourage them down this path.

Percentage of Private Issuers Across EM Corporate Credit

Theme 3: The Global Rise of Technology Sector Issuance

The hype surrounding AI not only affects corporate credit markets through the impact on an issuer’s productivity and operations but also the financing needed for its growth. Hyperscaler capex estimates rose throughout 2025, with expectations of more than $600 billion in 2026 across the major firms[4] and further future growth. In addition, supply estimates for 2026 are upward of $220 billion, or about 12% of total U.S. investment-grade (IG) issuance forecasts.

Massive cash generation from these companies will likely cover the majority of investment needs and shareholder returns (such as dividends and buybacks), yet it is prudent to consider what effects this issuance growth could have on our universe. We see three potential impacts affecting EM corporates: relative value, marginal crossover investor interest, and growth in EM technology issuance.

First, fixed-income valuation and portfolio management rely on the concept of relative value, where one instrument’s cash flow stream (coupons and principal), plus the uncertainty/volatility of this stream, is compared to another. If a significant amount of high-rated technology sector debt is issued, causing spreads to widen, then it is likely that other high-rated sectors will be affected by relative value comparisons, creating a waterfall of spread adjustments across ratings categories.

Second, crossover investors are market participants that typically invest in an asset class, but for a myriad of reasons will tactically or strategically allocate capital to another, crossing over into nondedicated areas. It is possible that significant U.S. technology sector issuance could make these investors reconsider allocations to some EMs.

Lastly, while capex from large Chinese technology issuers (such as Alibaba, Tencent, Baidu, and JD.com) in our universe is mildly increasing, their funding models look quite different compared to the United States. Chinese tech firms have much lower capex intensity for now and rely heavily on domestic onshore Chinese renminbi (CNY) markets, convertible bonds, banks, and policy lenders for funding. Our analysis suggests Chinese tech issuance is unlikely to experience a surge similar to that of the U.S. sector, and diversified domestic markets can absorb it.

AI Is Driving Higher Technology Sector Capex (In Billions)

Theme 4: Index Evolution and How Change Matters Over Time

Officially launched in 2007 (but backfilled to 2001), the CEMBI family of indices provides a widely used investment universe and performance benchmarks for EM corporate debt investors.

While the majority of peer funds are benchmarked to the J.P. Morgan CEMBI BD, the investable universe is better defined using the J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad[5], which currently comprises 729 issuers across 52 countries, with $1.2 trillion in market value. When looking at this index, its evolution is evident.

One can look at this progression from several angles. On a ratings basis, the high-yield (HY) component of the index peaked in 2018 at 44%. Since then, HY-rated issuer weights have declined to 34%, implying the IG component is now 66%. This ratings evolution should be considered for accurate historical spread and yield comparisons. It is the A-rated component that has experienced the largest growth, while both BBs and Bs have declined more than 5%. This improvement in index ratings should be considered for appropriate historical analysis.

Country weights, and therefore regional weights, have also changed over time. The biggest outlier is the decline in Asia, which peaked at 62% and is now at 44%. Latin America also declined, having peaked at 44% in 2011, and is currently 27%.

Of note, emerging Europe is now only 5% of the index, peaking at almost 25% in 2008, with the decrease predominantly driven by Russia’s exit from the index in 2022. Offsetting these declines is the rise of the Middle East, now 20% of the index, more than doubling in the last 10 years.

But the most notable weight change by country has been China. China’s weight in the index peaked at 27% in mid-2021 and has fallen to 14%. Greater China, which includes Hong Kong and Macau, was 36% of the index and is presently 26%. In Latin America, Brazil’s debt boom in 2012 took the country's weight to 25% but has been relatively stable at almost 10% since 2020.

When doing historical spread and yield analysis, we are keenly aware of index evolution and make the appropriate adjustments. Understanding our investable universe and its evolution is key to generating potential outperformance.

CEMBI Index Evolution

Theme 5: Valuation and Dispersion of Returns

As we begin 2026, index-level spreads appear historically low, suggesting low-risk premia in EM corporate credit and lower forward-looking returns. Further analysis is warranted.

Total returns in fixed income can be divided into two components: the risk-free return and the spread return. Since inception, returns for the U.S. dollar asset class have been approximately 6%, evenly split between risk-free (or Treasury rate) and excess return (or spread return).

With the duration of the asset class at four years, we currently see the risk-free yield at 3.8% and the spread at over 200 basis points (bps) for an index yield of 5.8%.

The breadth of instruments in the EM corporate credit asset class provides significant dispersion around average yearly returns. Chart 6 shows the range of returns with the index across time. We further analyze and rank returns by issuer and decile.

This return dispersion allows for true active management to add value above the beta of an asset class, and the larger the range, the more opportunity for active management.

It is evident that almost half of index issuers began 2026 with a yield below 5%. But it is also evident that there is a significant opportunity set of issuers yielding between 5% and 9%. Appropriately, navigating this still plentiful space could provide potential above-index returns without taking on excessive credit or unnecessary idiosyncratic risks.

Decile Dispersion In EM Corporate Credit

Comparing Yield Dispersion In 2024 and 2025

[1] The J.P. Morgan CEMBI BD is a market capitalization-weighted index that tracks the total returns of U.S. dollar-denominated debt instruments issued by corporate entities in EM countries. (Index information has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable but J.P. Morgan does not warrant its completeness or accuracy. The indices are used with permission. The indices may not be copied, used, or distributed without J.P. Morgan’s prior written approval. Copyright 2025, JPMorgan Chase & Co. All rights reserved.) Index performance is provided for illustrative purposes only. Indices are unmanaged and do not incur fees or expenses. A direct investment in an unmanaged index is not possible. [2] A nonlinear curved relationship between two variables that denotes acceleration. [3] Full-cycle cost includes finding and development costs, operating expenses, royalties, taxes, cost of capital, and overhead. [4] Includes Bloomberg estimates for Microsoft, Meta, Google, Amazon, and Oracle. [5] The J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad is a widely used benchmark that measures the performance of U.S. dollar-denominated corporate bonds issued by entities in EM countries.

Original Post

data.title: Domino's Pizza: One Of The Few Winners In A Tough Restaurant Market - And Now Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction

Domino's Pizza (DPZ) is now down to some of the lowest levels in over half a decade, despite the company proving its resilience and growing at a time when most peers are shrinking.

I rate DPZ a Buy, backed by their strong brand, value proposition, attractive valuation, and significant potential to grow domestically and internationally, with high-quality operations and a supply chain that offers a strong moat, even though macro pressure can continue to affect their valuation in the foreseeable future.

Strong Foundation Shining Despite Pressure

Unlike most peers, despite the ongoing macro pressure, DPZ reported a strong 2025, growing their domestic and international store count alongside a 5.4% annual increase in global retail sales (4.8% US, 5.9% international), while the free cash flow grew by 31.2% despite a 6.8% increase in CAPEX.

This ultimately comes down to several factors, including their strong brands, marketing, and the massive supply chain that offers a major competitive advantage, operating 22 regional dough manufacturing and supply chain centers, 2 thin crust facilities, and 1 vegetable processing center in the US as of the end of 2025, with DPZ mentioning the following during their latest 10-K report:

We believe our franchisees voluntarily choose to obtain food and other products from us because we offer the most efficient, convenient and cost-effective alternative, while also offering both quality and consistency. Our supply chain segment offers profit-sharing arrangements to U.S. and Canadian franchisees who purchase all of their food for their stores from our centers. These profit-sharing arrangements generally offer participating franchisees and Company owned stores with 50% of the pre-tax profit from our supply chain center operations. We believe these arrangements strengthen our ties to and provide aligned benefits with franchisees.

Meanwhile, both JPMorgan and BTIG highlighted DPZ's undervaluation not long ago regardless of the CEO transition, stating that the ongoing weakness seen from Pizza Hut (YUM) and Papa John's (PZZA) recently that's likely going to last for long can lead to another cycle of market share gain for DPZ, especially given their aggressive marketing and their significant competitive advantages.

After adding 776 net stores in 2025, Domino's expects to add over 800 net stores in 2026, with global retail sales to grow by about 6% in 2026, with about 3% comp growth in the US as they grow their market share (despite expecting the environment to remain pressured in 2026), while their operating income would grow by 8%, expecting $120 million in CAPEX that would revert to $110 million in 2027.

Financially, based on DPZ's latest report, we see an overall solid position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities while also reducing their leverage to 4.4x in 2025 compared to 4.9x in 2024, which is not the best, but it remains manageable and spread well over the years, recently refinancing $1 billion worth of debt into $500 million 5-year notes with a 4.93% interest rate and $500 million 7-year notes with a 5.217% interest rate.

Recently, DPZ also announced a significant ~14.4% dividend increase, getting to a yield of about 2.29% based on current prices, representing about $270.3 million per year, for a ~40.25% payout ratio based on last year's FCF, plus another $236.86 million spent on buybacks last year, which remain covered by their cash flow.

DPZ's next earnings report will be released on April 27th, with the market expecting $1.17 billion in revenue and $4.31 in EPS. I don't expect them to deliver a significant surprise given the environment, but the company's performance will likely be solid nonetheless, as their recent actions should help capture some market share while competitors report issues. I'm also looking forward to comments regarding the ongoing macro uncertainty, and even though I wouldn't expect some major change in their long-term approach, they are known to adapt to the environment, so there is the possibility of a change in guidance, in my opinion.

Macro Risks Rising

Even before the ongoing conflict in Iran, the consumer environment was far from great, with many companies reporting issues, especially in the US, with what started as natural pressure from rate hikes being accentuated by tariffs, rising levels of financial stress, and significant trade-downs, which affected many restaurant chains alike, as these companies were also affected by higher borrowing costs, supply chain disruptions, and inflationary pressure (workforce/ingredients/etc.).

Right now, every extra day of uncertainty in Iran adds significant levels of pressure that goes beyond just the major jumps in oil and gas that are reflected in pump prices, with the risk of a global slowdown or even recession rising, and even though I don't expect this to last for too long given reasons detailed before, a recovery will not be instant, meaning that we're almost guaranteed to see a hit to consumer budgets that can delay a potential turnaround in the industry - and that's in a good scenario where this conflict ends fast.

This environment can also make it tougher for DPZ to continue offering the same levels of quality, value, and margins without having to sacrifice at least one, as we're talking about something that the Fed and ECB recently warned could have significant inflationary effects on the global economy.

Since the role of rate hikes is to fight this inflation in the first place, it wouldn't be impossible to see the Fed (regardless of who the next Chair will be after Powell's term ends in May) keep rates higher for longer or even consider hiking them again, with the ECB reportedly being ready to do it almost immediately even if this inflation surge is short-lived, recently pushing European borrowing rates to 15-year highs.

Especially given their leverage, higher rates or elevated levels for longer are ultimately a significant risk, as they risk adding higher levels of interest with every refinancing.

Something to keep in mind is that an international expansion is not necessarily a magical tool for growth, with the company already having more than twice as many international stores (franchised) as domestic ones, although these generate a fraction of the income generated by their US operations since they're only receiving royalties.

However, an advantage they have is the low royalty/take rate compared to peers, which sacrifices some of their royalty income but can allow them to expand and prove their resilience even during a weak environment like this, as the franchisees can deal with the ongoing macro pressure more efficiently, while other fast-food chains are even forced to go into store closures and rethink their approach [see Wendy's (WEN)].

Risks Reiterated

Although we're talking about a solid and well-run business with an excellent brand that made even Warren Buffett invest in it, they are not immune to market weakness, and given the ongoing Iran conflict and potential to see more escalations or pressure even if there's a quick resolution, it can certainly take longer to see a turnaround than we might expect at first, which can also affect their valuation.

Meanwhile, competition remains a major fundamental issue of this industry, as we're talking about much more than just several other pizza restaurants (which is very common and simple to copy even for a small business), especially following the rise of delivery apps, which makes it easier for everyone to unlock the major potential of home delivery, which used to be an advantage specific to pizza back in the day.

Valuation

Starting from DPZ's $671.5 million FCF in 2025 and assuming no changes in net borrowings since they can continue refinancing their debt from these levels, I'll assume a 10% CAGR over the first 5 years, followed by a 7.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5, below the market's EPS estimates and possible thanks to their long-term target of reaching ~50,000 stores globally, with potential from macro/supply chain improvements alongside it, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate, a discount rate of 10% to demand a solid margin of safety, as well as 33.958 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $14.67 billion, for an intrinsic value of $432.11 per share, above the current $352.94 despite using relatively conservative estimates.

Conclusion

DPZ is a Buy, backed by their strong brand and prudent management, proving their resilience and potential to grow even in a weak environment, being in a great position to capture more market share.

There are risks to remember given the industry, macro factors, and levels of debt, but the overall risk-reward seems to be in the investment's favor at current levels, as this valuation shows a significant discount compared to the company's potential.

data.title: Amphenol: A Blue-Chip Compounder With Sustainable Competitive Advantages And AI Tailwinds
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Value Vest as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

I consider Amphenol Corporation (APH) to be one of the highest-quality industrial technology companies in the public markets today. I base this belief on three factors: its entrenched competitive position based on switching costs and engineering know-how; its decentralized operating model, which generates superior margins across every part of the cycle; and its disciplined acquisition engine, which helps continually to expand the company's addressable market while avoiding margin dilution.

This secular growth driver is an additional tailwind to the already resilient and diversified revenue streams of the business in the face of the ramp-up of AI data center expansion. I have used a valuation model to arrive at a fair value of approximately $170 per share; this implies around 39x my 2026E adjusted EPS estimate, a valuation I believe is justified by the franchise durability.

Company Overview

Amphenol makes ultra-high-performance electrical connectors, sensors, and interconnect systems for situations where failure is not an option (avionics, electric-vehicle battery management, military weapon systems, power grids, hyperscale data centers).

The company benefits from a diversified product and end-market base, none of which by itself accounts for more than ~25% of sales, providing a natural hedge against cyclicality in any particular vertical. Organizationally, Amphenol is organized along the lines of more than 140 autonomous general managers (GMs) around the world, each of whom has full responsibility for pricing, cost controls, and customer responsiveness within their operations.

This relatively flat organization allows Amphenol to operate like a collection of small businesses while still reaping the scale benefits of a large company (global supply chain, capital equipment economies, cross-selling). Over the past several years, its aggressive acquisition program has allowed it to accelerate its organic growth.

Competitive Position and Durable Advantages

I believe Amphenol has a broad, sustainable competitive advantage based on two reinforcing sources. The most significant and defensible is customer switching costs since their connectors live inside mission-critical systems (ADAS modules, satellite communications, power-grid infrastructure) where an electrical failure is a harbinger for personal injury, death, or cascading blackouts. OEMs in these markets are extremely risk-averse.

Though they may go elsewhere for the next generation of a software package or power supply, once system designers have selected and integrated a supplier like Amphenol, the monetary and time costs of switching are simply too high: full system design updates, requalification testing, retraining engineering teams, and the risk that the cheaper component is simply the one that fails in the field are simply too great. Indeed, Amphenol often develops engineering teams on-site at OEM plants during the early stages of platform design, laying the foundation for enduring personal and institutional relationships.

Another source of competitive advantage lies in the proprietary design and engineering capabilities embodied in Amphenol's intangible assets. The addition of value creation through customization is one of the company's key offerings to customers: almost every connector that Amphenol sells is custom or semi-custom, adapted to the specific conditions of its application.

Amphenol is fortunate to have a large existing library of connector and sensor designs from which it can reuse or adapt in different configurations, and management states that a fairly significant proportion of current revenue derives from products launched two to three years ago, indicating a history of continuous product refresh. In fields such as high-speed backplanes for cloud data centers, high-voltage EV architectures, and next-generation military electronics, requirements are changing quickly, and it is important to be a partner in the system design rather than a simple component supplier.

What unites these advantages aside from the stronger pricing power each delivers is Amphenol's cost discipline. It's a company that combines some pricing power with a localized production base, relatively heavy outsourcing, and a significant Asian assembly footprint in lower-cost countries. The net result has been operating margins above most of its peers generally and more stable in cyclical downturns than one might expect; the margins have held up far better than a typical cyclical industrial. To me, a stable margin structure in a cyclical play is to be expected in a naturally structurally advantaged business.

Peers

The interconnect market in which Amphenol operates is fiercely competitive and faces its biggest competitors on its 'home' turf - TE Connectivity (TEL), Molex, and Samtec - each has their own particular strengths.

TE Connectivity is the largest direct competitor of Amphenol and the world's largest connector producer by revenue, especially strong in automotive, where market share is most concentrated. TE Connectivity has both a huge scale advantage and strong ongoing OEM relationships; however, Amphenol consistently has a higher operating margin and is a more nimble organization on account of its decentralized structure. TE has greater exposure to the cyclical automotive market, which could be a hindrance in times of weak demand, whereas Amphenol's wider end markets provide stability.

Molex (Koch Industries) is a large, privately owned rival with robust positions in consumer electronics, data communications, and the automobile. Enabled by the coffers of its owners, Koch Industries, Molex is free to plow resources into the highest growth areas, such as high-speed data and optical interconnects, without the quarterly profit pressure suffered by Amphenol. Unlike Amphenol, Molex does not benefit from public share listing transparency and has fewer footholds in the defense and aerospace sectors.

Samtec, a family-owned, high-speed connectivity specialist for data centers, AI infrastructure, and comms, specializes in high-density connector technology and is reputed to be a go-to, highly technical expert for design support, a key characteristic attributed to Samtec in comparison to close rival Amphenol. Samtec also has a dedicated engineering team and is known for its expertise in signal integrity, although as a smaller business, it has a much more limited manufacturing presence.

Financial Analysis and Valuation

Amphenol finished 2025 on a very strong note: 4Q2025 revenue was approximately $6.44 billion, up more than 49% year over year, with adjusted EPS of $0.97 and operating margin in the high 20s.
